revision.text:
[[ja:備後護国神社]] {{Citation needed|date=2017-08-28|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Bingo Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Bingo gokoku-jinja Haiden.jpg|250px|Bingo Gokoku Shrine Haiden]] | caption = Haiden | map_type = Japan Hiroshima | latd = 34 | latm = 29 | lats = 33.8 | latNS = N | longd = 133 | longm = 21 | longs = 36.4 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 1-9-1 Marunouchi, Fukuyama City, Hiroshima Prefecture 720-0061 | deity = Spirits of war dead from Bingo Province
Ōhiko-no-Mikoto
Takeminakata-no-Mikoto
Toyomikata-no-Mikoto | founded = 1868 | features = Formerly Abbe Shrine | annual_festival = Second Saturday of May, October 23 | website = [https://bingo-gokoku.jp Bingo Gokoku Shrine] }} {{nihongo|'''Bingo Gokoku Shrine'''|備後護国神社|1=Bingo Gokoku Shrine|Bingo Gokoku Jinja|qq=}} is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|福山市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyama, Hiroshima|qq=}}’s {{ill|Marunouchi|en|Marunouchi|qid=Q1196955|lt=Marunouchi|1=Marunouchi}} district, north of {{ill|Fukuyama Castle (Bingo Province)|en|福山城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyama Castle (Bingo Province)|qq=}}. The shrine is designated as a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} and was formerly under the designation of "Abbe Shrine". The deities enshrined include the spirits of war dead from Bingo Province, Ōhiko-no-Mikoto, Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Toyomikata-no-Mikoto, and successive lords of {{ill|Fukuyama Domain|en|福山藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyama Domain|qq=}}, including {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|阿部正弘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Masahiro|qq=}}. == History == The shrine was initially established in 1868 by the lord of Fukuyama Domain, {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|阿部正弘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Masahiro|qq=}}, to enshrine those who died in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|戊辰戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boshin War|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hakodate War|en|箱館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|qq=}}. Originally founded as a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokonsha|qq=}} in the village of Yoshitsumura, it was relocated in 1893 to its current location in the Honmaru area of Fukuyama Castle and renamed "Kanpei Fukuyama Shōkonsha" in 1901. In 1939, it was designated by the Minister of the Interior as "Fukuyama Gokoku Shrine". During World War II, plans were made to expand the shrine grounds to include a large precinct and sports ground. Despite continued construction during the war, the main hall was destroyed during the bombing of Fukuyama on August 8, 1945. After the war, it was briefly named "Bingo Shrine" and integrated with the original Fukuyama Shōkonsha. In 1954, it was decided to integrate the shrine with Abbe Shrine’s main hall and the shrine was renamed "Bingo Gokoku Shrine" in 1957. The shrine’s grounds include the historic "Musashi Meditation Stone", believed to have been used by Miyamoto Musashi during his time in Fukuyama. == Miyamoto Musashi and Fukuyama == {{multiple image | image1 = Bingo gokoku-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption1 = Haiden and Honden (side view) | image2 = Bingogokoku-jinja Shinmon.JPG | caption2 = Shrine gate | image3 = Bingogokoku-jinja04s1800.jpg | caption3 = Musashi Meditation Stone }} The shrine grounds house the "Koshikake Stone", associated with Miyamoto Musashi, a legendary swordsman. According to legend, Musashi rested on this stone while visiting the estate of Nakayama Shōgen, a retainer of the Mizuno family, around 1624-1629. The stone was later moved to Abbe Shrine and finally to Bingo Gokoku Shrine. == Meiji Restoration and Tenjin-yama == The shrine sits on Tenjin-yama, which played a defensive role during the Bakumatsu period. The hillside was fortified by Fukuyama Domain to defend against possible Chōshū Domain attacks. Although the original defensive structures have been altered, remnants like the Akamon gate remain within the shrine grounds. == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Fukuyama Station|en|福山駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyama Station|qq=}} North Exit * 20 minutes by car from the {{ill|Sanyo Expressway|en|山陽自動車道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanyo Expressway|qq=}}’s Fukuyama East or Fukuyama West Interchange == Surrounding Area == * {{ill|Fukuyama Castle (Bingo Province)|en|福山城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyama Castle (Bingo Province)|qq=}} * Fukuyama Hachiman Shrine * Sanzō Inari Shrine * Fukuyama Budokan * Fukuyama Human Rights Peace Museum * Fukuyama Art Museum * Fukuyama Literature Museum * Hiroshima Prefectural History Museum * Fukujukai Pavilion == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * ''Fukuyama Castle (11 Edition)'', Fukuyama Cultural Property Association, 2006 == External Links == * [https://bingo-gokoku.jp Bingo Gokoku Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal|qq=}} {{Stub|Shinto|qq=}} {{Gokoku Shrines|qq=}} {{Beppyo Shrines|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|備後護国神社|version=102011107|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:備後護国神社]] {{Commons category|Bingo-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11387373}} [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1868]] [[Category:Fukuyama Domain]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hiroshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fukuyama, Hiroshima]] [[Category:福山市の歴史]] [[Category:箱館戦争]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]
